content:
A sequential scan is usually a cost decision, not an act of database rebellion.

7 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

You created the index.
Postgres can see the index.
The query still performs a sequential scan.
The usual reaction is to blame the planner. The useful reaction is to ask what the planner believes: how many rows will match, how expensive the heap fetches will be, and whether your predicate can use that index at all.
An index is an option. It is not an instruction.
Postgres is minimizing estimated cost, not honoring intent
For each query, PostgreSQL considers possible execution paths and chooses the plan with the lowest estimated cost. The official EXPLAIN documentation describes plans as trees of nodes and identifies the top node’s estimated total cost as the value the planner generally seeks to minimize.
That word — estimated — explains most surprises.
Suppose a filter matches a large share of a table. An index scan may need to traverse index pages, discover many tuple locations, and fetch heap pages that are scattered across storage. A sequential scan reads the table in physical order and checks each row.
The index exists. The sequential path can still be cheaper.
Small tables make this especially obvious. PostgreSQL’s documentation notes that a table occupying one disk page will almost always be read sequentially. The page must be fetched either way; reading an additional index adds work.
At the other extreme, a broad predicate on a large table can also favor a sequential scan. If the query needs much of the table, random-looking heap access through an index is not automatically a shortcut.
There are also middle paths. PostgreSQL can use a bitmap index scan to collect matching tuple locations, then read heap pages more efficiently. “Index scan or sequential scan” is not the planner’s only choice.
Read the chosen node as a decision:
→ Seq Scan: estimated cheapest to read and filter the table
→ Index Scan: estimated cheap enough to walk the index and fetch rows
→ Index Only Scan: estimated able to satisfy output from the index, subject to visibility checks
→ Bitmap Index Scan + Bitmap Heap Scan: estimated useful to batch heap access
The planner is not asking whether an index exists. It is asking whether a usable index path wins the cost comparison for this query and this data.
First find the lie in estimated rows
Start with evidence:
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT id, created_at, status
FROM orders
WHERE tenant_id = 42
AND status = 'pending';EXPLAIN shows the estimate. ANALYZE executes the statement and adds actual timing and row counts. BUFFERS shows buffer activity.
Be careful with writes: EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually runs the statement. Wrap a write in a transaction and roll it back when appropriate, or test safely elsewhere.
Now compare estimated rows with actual rows at the suspicious scan node.
If PostgreSQL estimated 200 rows and actually processed 200, the statistics are probably not the main problem. The sequential scan may genuinely be cheaper.
If it estimated 200 and actually processed 200,000, you found a planning input that is wrong.
Run ANALYZE on the relevant table:
ANALYZE orders;PostgreSQL stores planner statistics such as row counts, page counts, null fractions, common values, distinct-value estimates, histograms, and correlation. They are approximations, and some catalog values are not updated continuously.
Skew can defeat a sample. A status column might be mostly completed, with a small but operationally important pending population. A low statistics target may not describe the distribution well enough.
Increase the target on the column that needs better resolution, then analyze again:
ALTER TABLE orders
ALTER COLUMN status SET STATISTICS 500;ANALYZE orders;A higher target increases planning-statistics detail and analysis cost. Do it selectively. “Set every column to the maximum” is not tuning.
Multi-column correlation creates another common lie. PostgreSQL normally has single-column statistics and can misestimate combined conditions when columns are dependent. A tenant and region, country and currency, or order state and completion timestamp may not be independent.
Use extended statistics for relationships the planner needs to understand:
CREATE STATISTICS orders_tenant_status_stats
(dependencies, mcv)
ON tenant_id, status
FROM orders;ANALYZE orders;The planner statistics documentation is explicit: PostgreSQL does not automatically compute every possible multivariate combination. You create statistics objects for combinations that matter.
Do not add an index to fix a selectivity lie. Fix what the planner believes first.
If this is the class of problem currently burning production time, PostgreSQL War Room: 10 Fix Patterns for Lock Contention, Connection Exhaustion & Missing Indexes puts the emergency queries, EXPLAIN ANALYZE workflow, and production diagnosis paths in one place.
Then prove the predicate matches the index
An index can be healthy and irrelevant.
Start with column order. A B-tree index on (tenant_id, created_at) is naturally useful for predicates that constrain the leading tenant_id column. A query filtering only on created_at may not get the path you expected.
Then inspect expressions and types.
This index:
CREATE INDEX users_email_idx ON users (email);does not directly represent lower(email). If the workload searches normalized values, use a matching expression index:
CREATE INDEX users_lower_email_idx ON users (lower(email));The query expression must match the indexed expression closely enough for PostgreSQL to recognize the path.
Partial indexes have a stricter rule. If you create:
CREATE INDEX orders_pending_idx
ON orders (tenant_id, created_at)
WHERE status = 'pending';the query’s conditions must imply the partial-index predicate at planning time. A generic prepared statement with status = $1 may not prove that implication because the parameter could be something else.
Check operators and casts too. A cast on the indexed column, incompatible comparison semantics, a leading-wildcard pattern, or a function wrapping the column can make the expected B-tree path unusable.
Do not ask, “Is there an index on this column?”
Ask:

• Does the access method support this operator?

• Does the predicate constrain the leading index columns?

• Does the expression match?

• For a partial index, can the planner prove the predicate?

• Does the query need enough rows that the index still loses?
Existence is the least interesting property of an index.
Test the planner’s cost model without hard-coding a fantasy
PostgreSQL exposes planner cost settings. They are powerful and easy to misuse.
random_page_cost represents the estimated cost of a non-sequential page fetch relative to other planner costs. effective_cache_size tells the planner how much cache may be available to a query. It does not allocate memory.
The query-planning configuration documentation notes that a higher effective_cache_size makes index scans more likely, while a lower value makes sequential scans more likely. Hardware, caching, concurrency, and storage behavior all affect sensible settings.
Inspect before changing:
SHOW random_page_cost;
SHOW seq_page_cost;
SHOW effective_cache_size;For diagnosis, disable sequential scans only in the current session:
SET LOCAL enable_seqscan = off;Then run the same EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) inside a transaction and compare.
This is an experiment, not a fix.
The PostgreSQL index-usage documentation recommends forced-plan testing to determine whether the planner is right or whether its cost estimates do not reflect reality. If the forced index plan is slower, the planner saved you from your index. If it is consistently faster under representative conditions, investigate statistics and cost assumptions.
Do not set enable_seqscan = off globally. PostgreSQL can still choose a sequential scan when no alternative exists, and suppressing a valid plan type can make other queries much worse.
Do not tune from a warm-cache laptop against a tiny copy of production data. Plan choices can change with table size, distribution, cache state, parameters, and concurrency.
Test the real shape of the workload.
Fix the cause, then verify across parameter values
There is no universal “make Postgres use my index” command worth deploying.
The fix depends on the diagnosis:

• Stale statistics → run ANALYZE and verify autovacuum/auto-analyze behavior

• Skewed distribution → raise the statistics target selectively

• Correlated predicates → create extended statistics

• Wrong index shape → change column order or add an appropriate expression/partial index

• Too many matching rows → accept the sequential or bitmap plan

• Inaccurate cost assumptions → calibrate cautiously against representative measurements

• Prepared-statement sensitivity → compare plans across common and extreme parameter values
After the change, capture the plan again:
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, SETTINGS)
SELECT id, created_at, status
FROM orders
WHERE tenant_id = 42
AND status = 'pending';Verify more than elapsed time. Check estimated versus actual rows, loops, rows removed by filters, heap fetches for index-only scans, buffer reads and hits, temporary I/O, and the effect of LIMIT.
Then test a common tenant, a tiny tenant, and a tenant with unusual data. The perfect index plan for a selective value can be a terrible plan for a value matching half the table.
The goal is not an Index Scan badge.
The goal is a correct plan model that chooses well as data changes.
For a compact reference to index design, anti-patterns, joins, pagination, and reading plans, keep SQL Performance Cheatsheet beside your next tuning session.
And if you want the planner to interrogate you for a change, Froquiz includes 10,000+ questions across SQL, Docker, Git, AWS, and other engineering topics, with scenario-based Senior Dev Challenges rather than syntax-only drills.
→ Froquiz
